data:
9th Grade. The entry point into full participation in the life of the church.
Let’s be very clear about what Confirmation is — and is not:
It is not graduation from church. It is not a gold star for attending. It is not a hoop to jump through so parents get the photo. It is three things, all at once:
God confirms a youth’s baptismal identity as a beloved child of God. The youth confirms their faith in Jesus Christ — not their parents’ faith, not their grandmother’s faith, their own. Everyone confirms that this is the beginning, not the end. Confirmation works best when it’s relational, not informational. A student will forget most of the doctrine by Thanksgiving. They won’t forget the adult who knew their name, asked their hard questions, and prayed for them by name on Confirmation Sunday.
data.series#Series: 🛠️ Still under construction. A full Confirmation curriculum is coming. In the meantime, if your church has a framework that works — the Apostles’ Creed, the Westminster Shorter Catechism, the Heidelberg, or something homegrown — please contribute it.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned curriculum:
Who Is God? — The Trinity without the word salad Who Is Jesus? — Looking squarely at the claims, the cross, and the resurrection Who Am I? — Identity as a baptized child of God What Do We Do Now? — Worship, Word, sacraments, and service as a way of life The Credo Project — each student writes their own “I believe” statement to share on Confirmation Sunday

data:
Grades 9–12. The ages that ask the hardest questions, call the worst bluffs, and will spot spiritual BS at 400 yards. So we try not to serve any.
HS lessons on Opensource Sunday are built for students who are done being entertained and ready to be engaged. The template is the same as Middle School — Behind the Curtain → Center Stage → Prop Department → Directing the Scene — but with heavier discussion prompts, more Scripture per session, and fewer snack-based metaphors (though the snacks themselves remain non-negotiable).
data.series#Series: 🚧 Nothing to show here yet. The HS curriculum is still being drafted. If you’ve got a series you want to contribute — or a lesson you’ve already taught that killed — open a PR.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned series include:
Romans Road Trip — an 8-week walk through Paul’s letter to Rome Hard Sayings of Jesus — 6 weeks on the verses nobody puts on a coffee mug The Psalms, for People Who Aren’t Fine — 5 weeks in the raw honesty of the Psalms Apologetics Without the Argument — 4 weeks on doubt, evidence, and faith Stay tuned. Or, better yet, write one yourself.

data:
Grades 6–8. The ages most adults pretend they forgot. The ages that, according to the Barna Group, are when nearly 64% of lifelong Christians first made a commitment to Christ. Coincidence? Probably not.
These lessons are written for the real middle schooler — the one who laughs too loud, then goes quiet when it matters, then asks a question that’ll make you rethink your whole theology on the car ride home.
data.series#Series: Sermon on the MountFour weeks in the Beatitudes. Jesus flips the scoreboard.
data.what-to-expect-from-an-ms-lesson#What to:
Every lesson on this site follows the same template so volunteers aren’t left guessing:
Behind the Curtain — what the teacher needs to know before walking in Center Stage — the ONE thing you want students to walk out with Prop Department — the shopping list Directing the Scene — the actual plan: prayer, hook, Scripture, discussion, and a tangible “turn toward action” All MS lessons target ~45–60 minutes and are written assuming a group of 6–15 students, family-style. Scale the activities to your room.

data: A four-week series through the Beatitudes. Scripture anchor: Matthew 5:1–12 Series ONE thing: Jesus flips the world’s scoreboard upside down — the people the world overlooks are the ones God calls blessed.
data.jump-to-a-lesson#Jump to a lesson: Lesson 1 · Up the MountainMatthew 4:23–5:5 · Who gets called blessed? Lesson 2 · Blessed Are the HungryMatthew 5:6–8 · What’s underneath Lesson 3 · Blessed Are the BraveMatthew 5:9–12 · When it costs something Lesson 4 · Living the Blessed LifeMatthew 5:1–12 · Jesus in every line Leading this series? The four lessons are designed to build on each other — don’t skip Lesson 1 even if it feels like “just setup.” The scoreboard-flip is the whole hinge.
data.week-1--up-the-mountain#Week 1 — Up the: The setting. The crowd. The first three Beatitudes. Jesus starts his most famous sermon by cheering for all the wrong people.
data.week-2--blessed-are-the-hungry#Week 2 —: What’s underneath. Craving, mercy, and being the same person in every room.
data.week-3--blessed-are-the-brave#Week 3 — B: When following Jesus costs you something. Peacemakers and the persecuted.
data.week-4--living-the-blessed-life#Week 4 —: The reveal: every Beatitude is a portrait of Jesus himself. A commitment, a closing blessing, and sending.

data: Scripture: Matthew 4:23–5:5 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
By the end of Matthew 4, Jesus has become the most talked-about rabbi in the region. He’s been traveling all over Galilee, teaching in synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of sickness and pain. Crowds from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan are chasing him down. Then, in Matthew 5:1, he sees the crowd, walks up a mountainside, sits down (the posture of a teacher in that day), and begins the most famous sermon ever preached.
Before Jesus says a single word about how to live, he tells us who God is cheering for. The “Beatitudes” (from the Latin beatus — blessed, happy, flourishing) are not a checklist of behaviors to unlock God’s favor. They are Jesus announcing that God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world calls losers — the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek — God calls blessed. That would have sounded as scandalous then as it does now.
For middle schoolers, this is enormous. They live in a world where “blessed” almost always means rich, pretty, followed, talented, winning. Jesus just took a sharpie to that whole list.
This first lesson is the setup for the entire four-week series. We’re asking one big question: Who does Jesus say is blessed? Then we’ll walk through the first three Beatitudes — poor in spirit, those who mourn, the meek — so students can hear the pattern before we go deeper in the coming weeks.
Do our students actually believe Jesus when he says the people the world overlooks are the ones God calls blessed? Or do they, like the rest of us, secretly keep score the same way the world does?
data.catch-their-attention--the-scoreboard#Ca:
Before the lesson: Write across the top of the whiteboard in big letters: “WHO IS BLESSED?”
During the lesson: Say:
Raise your hand if you’ve ever heard someone say “I’m blessed” — on a post, in a song, on a jersey, anywhere. When people say that, what do they usually mean?
Let students shout out answers. Write them on the board in one color. (You’ll probably get: rich, healthy, popular, good family, winning, famous, lots of followers, talented, pretty, etc.)
Then say:
Okay — same question, but now in Jesus’ version. If Jesus wrote the list, who do you think he’d put on it?
Don’t give the answer yet. Let them guess. Write their guesses in a different color. Then say:
Hold onto that. Let’s read what Jesus actually says — and see how close we got.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world overlooks are the ones Jesus calls “blessed.”
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Looking back at our board — how different is Jesus’ list from what the world calls “blessed”? Who in your school do most people ignore or laugh at — who Jesus might actually be calling “blessed”? Have you ever felt like one of the people on Jesus’ list? (Poor in spirit, grieving, small, overlooked?) What would it mean to you to hear Jesus call you “blessed” right there? Why do you think Jesus started his biggest sermon with this list? What is he trying to do to us before he teaches us anything else?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers (two colors) Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening) Optional: printed photos of a trophy, a crown, a hospital bed, an empty lunch table, a kid sitting alone — anything that helps contrast “worldly blessed” with “Jesus blessed”
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-42355#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Over the next four weeks we’re going to be in the most famous sermon Jesus ever preached — something called the Sermon on the Mount. Three whole chapters of Jesus teaching us how to live in God’s kingdom. Tonight we’re reading the very beginning — who showed up, where Jesus sat down, and the very first words out of his mouth.
Read Matthew 4:23–5:5 together. Go around the circle; each student reads 1–2 verses. Anyone who doesn’t want to read can say “pass” — no pressure.
Discuss:
Before Jesus even says a word, what kind of crowd is showing up? (v. 23–25) — Sick people. Demon-possessed. People in pain. People from everywhere. Not the cool kids. Jesus sees this crowd, goes up the mountain, and sits down. Why does that matter? — In that day, a teacher stood to read the Scripture but sat to teach with authority. Jesus is about to drop something huge. Jesus uses the word “blessed” over and over. Based on these first three lines, how is his idea of “blessed” different from the one on our board? “Poor in spirit” — what do you think that means? — Spiritually empty-handed. Knowing you don’t have it all together. The opposite of proud. “Those who mourn” — why on earth would Jesus call grieving people blessed? — Because God is the God who comforts. Grief is not the end of the story for people who belong to him. “The meek” — is meek the same as weak? — Nope. Meek = strength under control. Like a warhorse that’s been trained. Think Moses. Think Jesus. Say:
Here’s what Jesus is doing. He is flipping the world’s scoreboard upside down. The world cheers for people who have it all together. Jesus cheers for people who know they don’t. The world runs away from people who are grieving. Jesus runs toward them. The world worships the loudest person in the room. Jesus blesses the one who doesn’t need to be.
For the next three weeks, we’re going to hear eight of these upside-down blessings. And they are going to change how we look at our school, our team, our lunch table — and our own hearts.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead a short popcorn prayer. Thank God for the gift of the group, and ask the Holy Spirit to open hearts to the words of Jesus tonight.
data.turn-toward-action--name-one#Turn Toward:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
On this card, write the name of one person in your life who the world doesn’t cheer for — someone who fits Jesus’ list more than the world’s list. You don’t have to show anyone. Just write the name.
Slip it in your pocket, your phone case, or your Bible. Your job this week: pray for that person every day. And do one thing — literally one thing — that tells them they matter. Say hi. Save them a seat. Text them. Invite them. Anything.
End with prayer. Invite students to pray — out loud or silently — for the name on their card. Close with:
Jesus, thank you that your blessing doesn’t depend on the scoreboard of the world. Give us your eyes this week. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:6–8 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Last week we set the stage: Jesus went up the mountain, sat down, and started flipping the world’s scoreboard upside down. He blessed the poor in spirit, those who mourn, and the meek. This week we move into the middle three Beatitudes — and they each zoom in on something inside us.
“Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled.” (v.6) — This is not a polite wish to be a better person. This is a gut-level craving for things to be right — right with God, right with ourselves, right in the world. Jesus is blessing the people who are starving for the things God cares about. “Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy.” (v.7) — Mercy isn’t just being nice. Mercy is giving someone what they don’t deserve because God gave us what we didn’t deserve. The merciful don’t keep score. They let go of the right to get even. “Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.” (v.8) — “Pure in heart” isn’t about never messing up. In the Old Testament, purity meant undivided — like pure gold, unmixed. Jesus is blessing people whose inside matches their outside. Same person on Instagram, in the hallway, at the dinner table, at Crossroads. Middle schoolers live in a world that tells them the outside is what matters — the fit, the follower count, the highlight reel. Jesus slides in and says the thing God is actually paying attention to is the inside — what you crave, how you treat people who can’t pay you back, and whether you’re the same person everywhere.
Are we hungry for the right things? Do we give mercy as freely as we want to receive it? Are we the same person when no one’s watching as when everyone is?
data.catch-their-attention--the-snack-thats-n:
Before the lesson: Set the covered tray of snacks on the middle of the table or floor, in plain sight, so students can smell it as they come in. Do not uncover it yet.
During the lesson: As students notice and start asking about the snacks, smile and say:
Yeah. They’re real. They’re here. But we’re not eating them yet.
Let the suspense build for a minute. Then ask:
What’s your favorite food you’d wait in line forever for? Stand in line for an hour. Drive for it. Beg your parents for it. What is it?
Let every student answer. Then ask:
Now, what if I told you Jesus once said the most blessed people in God’s kingdom are the ones who crave the right things as badly as you crave that food?
Say:
Tonight we’re reading three verses about what’s underneath — what we want, how we treat people, and who we really are. Then we’ll eat. Pinky promise.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
God cares about what’s underneath. The Beatitudes bless the people whose cravings, mercy, and integrity are shaped by him — not by the highlight reel.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Set up the three signs — HUNGER · MERCY · PURE HEART — on three different walls.
Discuss together:
What do middle schoolers hunger for the most? What are your friends craving? What are you craving? Who has shown you mercy that you didn’t deserve? What did it feel like? Who in your life needs mercy from you right now? Where in your life is it hardest to be the same person? Online vs. in person? At school vs. at home? With one friend vs. another? Why? Jesus says the pure in heart will “see God.” What do you think that actually means? — God shows up in places where we’re not hiding. The undivided heart gets to see him.
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A tray of snacks students actually want (pretzels, cookies, chips — something with smell), covered with a cloth napkin or foil Sticky notes or small slips of paper Pens/pencils Whiteboard or large paper + markers Christ light / candle + matches Three signs/printouts (one per wall): HUNGER · MERCY · PURE HEART
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-568#Read and:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Last week we started Jesus’ famous Sermon on the Mount. We heard him call the poor, the grieving, and the meek blessed. Tonight we’re reading the next three lines — and every one of them zooms in on what’s going on inside us. Let’s read together.
Read Matthew 5:6–8 together. Go around the circle; each student reads one verse. Anyone who doesn’t want to read can say “pass.”
Discuss:
Jesus uses hunger and thirst as a picture. Why those two? What do hunger and thirst feel like? — Urgent. Can’t ignore it. It takes over. “Hunger and thirst for righteousness” — what do you think that looks like in real life? — Actually wanting what God wants. Caring when something is unfair. Caring about your friend being hurt. Caring about your own heart. “Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy.” What’s the difference between being nice and being merciful? — Nice is polite. Mercy is giving someone what they don’t deserve — forgiveness, a second chance, a seat at the table — because God gave it to you first. “Pure in heart” — does that mean “perfect”? — No. It means undivided. Same person everywhere. No fake version online, no different version at home, no different version here. Just you. Which of these three is hardest for you — hungering for the right things, showing mercy, or being the same person in every room? Say:
Here’s the wild thing about these three. The world teaches us to curate the outside — the post, the fit, the bio, the team, the grade. Jesus walks in and asks: what’s underneath? What do you actually crave? How do you actually treat people who can’t do anything for you? Who are you when the camera’s off?
And here’s the promise in each one: those who hunger will be filled. The merciful will receive mercy. The pure in heart will see God. Jesus isn’t saying: get it right and then you’ll be blessed. He’s saying: if God is already changing what’s underneath, you’re already in the blessed life.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Popcorn prayer: invite each student to name one thing they’re thankful for in a single word.
data.turn-toward-action--pick-your-wall#Turn:
Hand out sticky notes. Say:
Here’s your move. Look at the three walls: Hunger. Mercy. Pure Heart. Pick the one where God is tapping you on the shoulder this week. Write on your sticky note one concrete thing you’re going to do — and stick it on that wall.
Examples: “Hunger” — I’ll read one chapter of Matthew this week. “Mercy” — I’ll forgive [name] and stop holding it over them. “Pure Heart” — I’ll delete/hide that thing I’m hiding. I’ll be the same person at school as I am here.
Give them time to write. Let them stick their notes up. Take a picture of each wall (Photographer role).
Uncover the snacks. Say:
I told you we’d eat. Here’s the thing — Jesus said the hungry will be filled. God doesn’t start this craving in us and then leave us starving. He promises to show up. So eat, and remember: the same God who filled you tonight is filling what’s underneath when you ask him to.
End with prayer. Thank God that he cares about what’s inside — and that he’s the one who fills it.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:9–12 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
We’ve watched Jesus flip the scoreboard (Lesson 1). We’ve watched him zoom in on the inside — hunger, mercy, pure heart (Lesson 2). Now he turns the camera outward. The last Beatitudes are about what the blessed life looks like when it costs you something.
“Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God.” (v.9) — There’s a huge difference between peace-keepers (people who avoid trouble by staying quiet) and peace-makers (people who walk into conflict to heal it). Jesus himself is the ultimate peacemaker — he didn’t keep his distance from our mess; he stepped right into it. Being called a “child of God” in this verse isn’t a generic title; it’s saying you look like your Father. Peacemakers carry the family resemblance. “Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.” (v.10) — This one stings. Jesus isn’t blessing people who are suffering because they’re being jerks. He’s blessing people who are paying a price for doing the right thing. Notice the payoff: same as the very first Beatitude — the kingdom of heaven. The series comes full circle. “Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you, and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.” (vv.11–12) — Jesus gets personal here. He shifts from “blessed are they” to “blessed are you.” And the response is not endure it or survive it — it’s rejoice and be glad. You’re in good company with every prophet who ever spoke up for God. Middle schoolers know what it costs to stand up. They know what it’s like to be made fun of, excluded, or called names for being “too churchy,” “too nice,” “too different.” Jesus isn’t minimizing that pain. He’s telling them: I see it. I’ve been there. And you are not alone — you are blessed.
What are we willing to stand for when it costs us something? And are we peacemakers — or just peacekeepers?
data.catch-their-attention--peacekeeper-or-pe:
Before the lesson: On the whiteboard, draw two columns: PEACE-KEEPER | PEACE-MAKER. Leave them blank.
During the lesson: Say:
There are two kinds of people in the world when things get uncomfortable. Peace-keepers and peace-makers. Let’s figure out the difference.
Ask the group to help you fill in each column. Prompt if they get stuck:
A peace-keeper does what to avoid a fight? (Stays quiet. Walks away. Pretends it’s fine. Agrees with whoever is loudest.) A peace-maker does what? (Steps in. Tells the truth in love. Apologizes first. Asks hard questions. Makes things right, not just quiet.) Then say:
Here’s the catch: peace-keeping is cheaper. Peace-making costs something — your reputation, your comfort, sometimes your friend group. Tonight Jesus is going to tell us which one he calls blessed. Spoiler: it’s not the cheap one.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
Following Jesus sometimes makes you less popular — and Jesus calls that blessed, because you’re in good company with the prophets and with him.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities--scenario-round:
Hand out the scenario cards (one per student or per pair). Say:
Read your card out loud. Then tell us: what would the peace-keeper do? What would the peace-maker do? What would it cost to be the peace-maker?
Walk through each one together. Don’t rush. Let the Shepherds in the room weigh in. Some of these will get real.
Extra discussion:
Can you think of a time you were a peacekeeper when you wish you’d been a peacemaker? What does it cost to stand up for someone at your school? What does it cost to stay silent? Is there someone in your life right now who you know God is nudging you to stand up for — or to make peace with?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers Scenario cards (prepped ahead — see below) A small bandage or band-aid for each student (for Turn toward Action) Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches Optional: a worship track ready to play softly at the end (something like “So Will I,” “Graves Into Gardens,” or “Stand in Your Love”)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-5912#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
For two weeks we’ve been in Jesus’ Sermon on the Mount. We’ve heard him bless the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek, the hungry, the merciful, and the pure in heart. Tonight we read the last Beatitudes — and they’re about what happens when living the blessed life actually costs you something.
Read Matthew 5:9–12 together, going around the circle.
Discuss:
“Peacemaker” vs. “peacekeeper” — which one is Jesus blessing? Why does it matter? Jesus says peacemakers will be called “children of God.” What do you think that means? — They look like their Father. God is a peacemaker — he stepped into our mess through Jesus. When we step into conflict to heal it, we carry the family resemblance. “Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness.” What does persecuted mean? Can you be persecuted in middle school? — Yes. Being made fun of, excluded, talked about, eye-rolled at — for doing what’s right. It’s real. Jesus says “rejoice and be glad.” How is that even possible when you’re being made fun of? — Because you’re in good company. You’re standing with every prophet who ever spoke up, and you’re standing with Jesus himself. You’re not crazy. You’re not alone. In verse 11, Jesus switches from “blessed are they” to “blessed are you.” Why do you think that matters? — He’s getting personal. He’s saying: I see you. I know what this costs. I’m calling YOU blessed. Say:
Here’s what Jesus is doing. He started this sermon by flipping the scoreboard. Now he’s closing the Beatitudes by telling us: when you actually live this way — when you’re hungry for the right things, showing mercy, being the same person everywhere, making peace, standing up for truth — it will cost you sometimes.
And when it does, don’t be shocked. Don’t be ashamed. Rejoice. You’re in the family. You look like Jesus. You’re standing where the prophets stood. Great is your reward in heaven.
data.scenario-cards#Scenario Cards:
Prep before class — write each on an index card:
Your friend group is roasting a kid who isn’t there. Everyone’s laughing. They look at you. You see a new kid eating lunch alone. Your table is already full of your best friends. Two of your friends are fighting and won’t talk to each other. Both are venting to you. Someone at school finds out you go to church and says, “So you actually believe that stuff?” A post goes around with a rumor about someone you barely know. Everyone’s sharing it. Your teammate is cheating off your test. They’d be really mad if you said anything.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to pray, or pray yourself — asking God for courage tonight.
data.turn-toward-action--the-band-aid#Turn To:
Give every student a bandage. Say:
Band-aids don’t prevent wounds. They cover them while they heal. Peacemakers are kind of like that — they don’t stop the world from being broken; they step into the broken places and help heal them.
Take this band-aid home. Stick it somewhere you’ll see it this week — your water bottle, your binder, your mirror. Every time you see it, ask yourself one question: “Who am I being called to make peace with or stand up for — right now?”
End with prayer. Play a worship track softly as you pray:
Jesus, thank you that you didn’t keep your distance from our mess. You stepped right into it. Make us peacemakers this week. Give us the guts to stand up when it costs us something. And remind us — when it’s hard — that we’re in your family, and we are blessed. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:1–12 (full Beatitudes) Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
This is the wrap-up. For three weeks, students have climbed the mountain with Jesus. They’ve heard him flip the scoreboard. They’ve looked underneath — at what they crave, how they give mercy, who they really are. They’ve been challenged to step into conflict and to stand up when it costs something.
Tonight we zoom all the way back out and read the whole Beatitudes as one piece. And here’s the big reveal that holds the whole series together:
Every single Beatitude is a portrait of Jesus himself.
He was poor in spirit — the King of glory born in a feeding trough. He mourned — he wept at Lazarus’ tomb, he wept over Jerusalem. He was meek — “gentle and humble in heart” (Matt. 11:29), riding on a donkey. He hungered and thirsted for righteousness — his food was “to do the will of him who sent me” (John 4:34). He was merciful — “Father, forgive them” (Luke 23:34). He was pure in heart — the sinless one. He was the ultimate peacemaker — he “made peace through his blood, shed on the cross” (Col. 1:20). He was persecuted — even to death on a cross. When Jesus said “blessed are…” he wasn’t giving us eight random categories. He was describing the shape of the life he was about to live — and inviting us into it.
The second big idea: the Beatitudes are bookended by the same promise. Verse 3 and verse 10 both end with “for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.” That’s Jesus’ way of saying: this isn’t pie-in-the-sky someday — you are already in the kingdom the moment you step into this way of life.
This lesson pulls the series together and asks each student: which Beatitude is God inviting you to lean into in this season of your life?
The goal tonight isn’t a new download. It’s a commitment. One Beatitude. One season. Lived out.
data.catch-their-attention--the-gallery-walk#:
Before the lesson: Post the eight Beatitude signs around the room — spread them out on walls, tables, even the floor. Have the room set up so students can walk around freely.
During the lesson: Say:
For three weeks we’ve been climbing this mountain together. Tonight we’re going to walk through all eight of the Beatitudes at once. Here’s what I want you to do:
Take a lap around the room. Read every sign. No talking yet. Just read.
When you’re done, come stand next to the Beatitude that hits you the hardest right now. Not the one that’s easiest. The one you need the most. The one God is nudging you toward.
Put on music. Let them walk. Give it at least 3–4 minutes. When most have landed somewhere, call the group back in.
Ask a few volunteers: Why did you land where you did? No one has to share, but some will want to. This is holy stuff — don’t rush it.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
The Beatitudes are the shape of Jesus’ own life. He’s not asking us to do anything he hasn’t already done — he’s inviting us to follow him into the blessed life, one step at a time.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Which Beatitude surprised you most over these four weeks? Why? Which Beatitude is God inviting you into right now, in this season of your life? Remember the three truths painted on our walls at Crossroads? “In Jesus Christ, you are loved, you belong, and you have been created to make a real difference.” How do the Beatitudes connect to those three? If you lived the Beatitudes out loud this year — at school, on your team, in your family — what do you think would change? About you? About them?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Eight printed signs — one for each Beatitude — posted around the room before students arrive Commitment cards (index cards or printed templates — see Turn toward Action) Pens/pencils (one per student) Whiteboard or large paper Christ light / candle + matches Worship music cued up (band or track — something reflective) Optional: a small card or printed Beatitudes list for each student to take home
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-5112#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Tonight we’re reading the whole thing — all eight Beatitudes — straight through. Listen for a pattern. Listen for where Jesus promises something. And listen for where you hear Jesus describing himself.
Read Matthew 5:1–12 together. One student reads it all, slowly, while everyone else listens with eyes closed. Or go around and have each student read one verse.
Discuss:
What stood out to you hearing all eight together? Do you notice the pattern? — Every one has the word “blessed” and every one has a promise. (Blessed are ___, for they ___.) Look at the first and last Beatitudes (verses 3 and 10). What do they have in common? — Same promise: theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Jesus bookends the whole list with it. He’s saying: this life isn’t a waiting game. You’re already in the kingdom. Here’s a wild question: which of these Beatitudes describes Jesus himself? Pause on that last one. Walk them through it together. Write on the whiteboard:
Poor in spirit → Born in a feeding trough. Mourned → Wept at Lazarus’ tomb. Meek → “Gentle and humble in heart.” Hungered for righteousness → “My food is to do the will of my Father.” Merciful → “Father, forgive them.” Pure in heart → The sinless one. Peacemaker → Made peace through the cross. Persecuted → All the way to the cross. Say:
Here’s the big thing I want you to see tonight. Jesus wasn’t giving us eight random rules. He was describing the shape of his own life. Every Beatitude is a portrait of him.
So when Jesus says “blessed are the poor in spirit…blessed are the merciful…blessed are the peacemakers…” he is literally saying: follow me into the blessed life. I’ve already lived it. Come with me.
That changes everything. This isn’t a list of impossible standards. It’s an invitation. Jesus is not standing at the top of the mountain yelling at us to climb. He’s climbing it with us.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite the whole group to join in a popcorn prayer — thank God for the last three weeks, ask him to speak clearly tonight.
data.the-eight-signs#The Eight Signs:
Write one per sheet, in large letters:
POOR IN SPIRIT — Theirs is the kingdom of heaven MOURNING — They will be comforted MEEK — They will inherit the earth HUNGRY FOR RIGHTEOUSNESS — They will be filled MERCIFUL — They will be shown mercy PURE IN HEART — They will see God PEACEMAKERS — They will be called children of God PERSECUTED FOR RIGHTEOUSNESS — Theirs is the kingdom of heaven
data.turn-toward-action--one-beatitude-one-se:
Hand every student a commitment card. Have a student read a template like this, or print one:
MY BEATITUDE
In this season, I believe God is inviting me into the Beatitude of ___________________________.
One thing I’m going to do this month to lean into it:
One person I’m going to ask to pray for me / walk with me in this:
Signed: ____________________________________
Give students space and quiet to fill it out. Play worship music.
Then say:
You get to keep this card. Put it in your Bible. Put it on your mirror. Put it in your locker. And find the person you wrote down and actually ask them — this week — if they’ll pray for you.
Close in a big circle. Invite students to hold the card as you pray:
Jesus, every single one of these Beatitudes is you. Thank you that you don’t just tell us how to live — you lived it first, all the way to the cross, all the way out of the grave. Thank you that your kingdom is already here for the poor in spirit, the mourners, the merciful, the brave. Help us follow you this season into the blessed life. Amen.
Close the series. Before students leave, stand at the door and say each student’s name and bless them:
"[Name], you are loved beyond your imagining. You belong no matter what. You have been created to make a real difference. Go be blessed."
That’s how we end this series. That’s the blessed life.
